content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 23, 1993

Curtis J. Trier
Occidental Petroleum Corporation
10889 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, Ca 90024

Re: Your Request For Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-93-398

Dear Mr. Trier:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice regarding
the campaign and lobbying disclosure provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because your questions are general in
nature, we treat your request as one for informal assistance
pursuant to Regulation 18329(2).2/

During our telephone conversation, you requested information
regarding the special odd-numbered year campaign reporting
requirements for state major donor committees.

Based on Section 84202.7, I advised you that during an odd-
numbered year such as 1993, committees which are not required to
file regular pre-election campaign statements must file the
special odd-year campaign reports if they make any number of
contributions totaling $5,000 or more to elected state officers,
their controlled committees (including an elected state officer's
controlled ballot measure committee), or committees primarily
formed to support or oppose any elected state officer during the
period covered by the special odd-year campaign report (January 1
through March 31 and July 1 through September 30).

I also stated that the special odd-year report was not
required if all of the contributions to elected state officers had
already been disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report (Form 635
or Form 645). (Section 84202.7(b).)

_____________________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91915. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Curtis Trier
I-93-398
Page 2

     However, effective January 1, 1994, Section 84202.7(b) has
been amended to eliminate the exception for contributions
disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report. (Chapter 218, Stats.
1993.)

     Therefore, beginning in 1995, the special odd-year report
will be triggered by contributions to elected state officers even
if the contributions have been disclosed on a lobbying disclosure
report.

     For example, Corporation "A" is a lobbyist employer and is
required to file quarterly lobbying disclosure statements. On May
25, 1995, Corporation "A" makes a $10,000 contribution to support
a city ballot measure and qualifies as a major donor committee.
On September 15, 1995, Corporation "A" makes a $2,500 contribution
to Assemblyman John Smith and a $2,500 contribution to Senator
Alice Jones. Because Corporation "A" made $5,000 in contributions
to elected state officers during the period only 1 through
September 30, the corporation must file a special odd-year
campaign report no later than October 31, 1995. In addition, the
corporation must report the same information on its lobbying
disclosure report due no later than October 31, 1995.

     The purpose of Section 84202.7 is to provide timely
information to the public concerning large contributions made to
elected state officers during non-election years when those
officeholders usually are required to file only semi-annual
campaign reports. (Section 84200.)

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Linda Moureaux
                                        Political Reform Consultant
                                        Technical Assistance Division

sections.question: During our telephone conversation, you requested information regarding the special odd-numbered year campaign reporting requirements for state major donor committees.
sections.conclusion: Based on Section 84202.7, during an odd-numbered year such as 1993, committees which are not required to file regular pre-election campaign statements must file the special odd-year campaign reports if they make any number of contributions totaling $5,000 or more to elected state officers, their controlled committees, or committees primarily formed to support or oppose any elected state officer during the specified periods (January 1 through March 31 and July 1 through September 30). Effective January 1, 1994, Section 84202.7(b) has been amended to eliminate the exception for contributions disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report. Therefore, beginning in 1995, the special odd-year report will be triggered by contributions to elected state officers even if the contributions have been disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the campaign disclosure requirements for major donor committees during odd-numbered years under Section 84202.7, and does the exception for contributions previously disclosed on lobbying disclosure reports still apply?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Major donor committees must file special odd-year campaign reports when contributing $5,000 or more to elected state officers during specified periods. As of January 1, 1994, the previous exception allowing contributions already disclosed on lobbying reports to be exempt from this requirement has been eliminated, so beginning in 1995, all qualifying contributions trigger the special report requirement regardless of prior lobbying disclosure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter responding to a telephone inquiry. The question and conclusion sections are synthesized from the body text as no formal Q&A section headers exist. Document provides specific statutory references (Section 84202.7) and an illustrative example. Marked as informal assistance per Regulation 18329(2), which does not provide immunity from liability.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: During our telephone conversation, you requested information regarding the special odd-numbered year campaign reporting requirements for state major donor committees.

CONCLUSION: Based on Section 84202.7, during an odd-numbered year such as 1993, committees which are not required to file regular pre-election campaign statements must file the special odd-year campaign reports if they make any number of contributions totaling $5,000 or more to elected state officers, their controlled committees, or committees primarily formed to support or oppose any elected state officer during the specified periods (January 1 through March 31 and July 1 through September 30). Effective January 1, 1994, Section 84202.7(b) has been amended to eliminate the exception for contributions disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report. Therefore, beginning in 1995, the special odd-year report will be triggered by contributions to elected state officers even if the contributions have been disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms that major donor committees must file special odd-year campaign reports when making contributions of $5,000 or more to elected state officers during specified periods, and clarifies that an amendment effective January 1, 1994, eliminates the prior exception for contributions already disclosed on lobbying disclosure reports.
